sentences.text: Yeah.
Well, in the morning we got up, and we had to stand on the appell until you got you-- you a policeman [? when ?] he took you to the-- to work.
And we got a little coffee, and a piece of bread.
I ate from [? two ?] [? pounds. ?] Every place wasn't anymore.
But I think you got used to it by then.
I think your inside was so strong that you didn't even need this either.
I didn't.
People went to work.
And everybody had their machines.
I worked-- my machine when I was making ammunition.
It really was a very important machine.
But I learned it very fast, and I didn't make any schmutz.
And--
Any schmutz, they called it.
I wasn't any [? wrongs ?] in it.
But if you made a lot, they took you away from it, and you never-- they never saw you again.
And we were there for a long time.
At night, when we went out when the work was finished, around 5 o'clock they took us back to the barracks, and we washed.
And we could go out socialize, really.
See the men.
A lot of them I know.
And I already knew my husband-- my husband what he is now.
And we talked--
We talked.
No, no, no.
No.
No husbands and wives.
They weren't such really people that old they should be married.
They were all young people.
Young, I mean 17, 16, 18, 19.
Very seldom, unless-- I don't know, they were there.
No, there were a few older people, too, but they were already there.
But from the people what I came from were all young people, very young people.
And-- [SIGHS]
No, nothing.
Nothing.
We lived by the day, every day the same thing.
We got up, we went to work, we came back.
I left in Skarzysko one thing, which I would want to say-- I would want to come back to it.
As we came to Skarzysko, there were already a lot of people where they lived in Skarzysko.
And there was a pregnant woman.
Everything Jews.
Yeah.
And she was pregnant.
When she was that time I think about four, five months.
But everybody knew that she's pregnant.
But there was a-- a doctor, a Jewish doctor.
And he told her right away, he said, so we made her a [GERMAN].
That means she was cleaning the barrack.
She-- we went away to work, and she left in the barrack, and she was cleaning the barrack.
And she was pregnant until she was nine months.
Came the night where she had to deliver the baby.
To be very honest I never saw delivering a-- delivering a baby, and I wanted to see, really.
We were all up.
It was dark.
And the doctor came in--
He came in because it was a hospital on the same premises.
This was in Skarzysko.
He was a Jewish doctor.
And he came in, he told her right away that we have to put the baby to sleep soon.
The baby comes out, because the baby will start screaming, and-- and they'll hear us.
She said fine.
And they delivered the baby, and he put something on the baby.
I don't know what he did.
And the baby was dead.
And she begged him, she says, you know what, give me the dead baby.
I want to feel-- I have-- I want to have the feeling from a mother for one minute.
And he did gave her the baby, and he took the baby away.
And that-- she-- she is alive, the same woman.
I don't know.
I didn't see her after the war anymore.
But she was liberated with us in Tschenstochau.
So I know that she is alive, that she was alive.
And she was a young girl, so she must be alive, unless-- I don't know what happened to her.
I had to tell this.
This is already in the good times.
Anyway, came January.
And it came January the-- the 16th.
This was the day we were liberated.
In the morning, they took us all out, and they put us in a-- on the same trains.
And we-- and in that time, you could sit-- they just pushed us in, men and women together, anything.
And I was pushed in with Jack and his four brothers.
We were all together, and my niece, in one wagon.
No.
No, nothing.
They told us that-- what we knew that the Russians are not very far, because we heard, like, shouts, shooting once in a while.
And they started, and we-- they put us in in the trains, and we were sitting there and sitting the whole day.
But someone said that he heard, like, a bomb was falling.
I didn't hear it.
When it came the evening, they opened up the trains, and they told us that they cannot take us now.
In the morning, we'll start marching.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they took us back into the-- we went in back to the barracks.
We didn't see a German for days.
But we heard shootings and bombs were falling, and the whole town was burning all around.
It seems that the Germans were very, very near.
The-- not the Germans, I'm sorry, the Russians.
The Russians really bombard-- knew about this camp, and they bombarded the [INAUDIBLE] what the trains were going.
And the trains could not go.
And this was very late at night, so they said they gonna go with us in the morning for a march.
We're gonna march to Germany.
To Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were-- we were exhausted.
All day without food and without water, sitting in those barracks.
And you don't know-- sitting in the--
--train, and not knowing what's going to happen.
So I went into the barrack, and I probably fell asleep.
Jack and his younger brother and [? anothers-- ?] we didn't hear about Germans, but we heard-- they heard.
I was asleep.
I really didn't know it.
I took off my shoes, and put them underneath my head, and made from the coat a pillow like I used day after day.
And I fell asleep.
And they said that somebody said that the Germans ran away, and we don't even have anybody.
So he said what will we wait?
Let's go and see it.
And there was like a little bridge from our camp to the place where the Germans lived.
And all three of them went over.
They started going slowly, and they see nobody is there, so they went a little bit further, until they came to the place where the Germans were.
And there was no one there.
They ran away, because the Russians were already so near, so they themselves ran away.
And they left, like, three probably empty-- to shoot, what you call it?
Rifles?
Rifles.
They all three took the rifles, and they came back into the camp.
And everybody picked them up, and everybody was crying.
It was like a joyous occasion.
And I was asleep.
So somebody came in and said, listen, your boyfriend just brought rifles in.
So I said, where?
I ran down without the shoes to look out the window.
And when I came back, somebody stole my shoes.
And I was sitting on the bridge and crying.
It was winter.
It was so cold.
It was January.
It was bitter cold.
How will I go out?
I had no shoes.
I was barefoot.
And then my husband came in, my husband now.
He said, why are you crying?
And he starts saying, we are liberated, I think.
I said, I have no shoes.
Somebody took my shoes.
So he said, you know what?
I think I saw laying shoes on the floor by the Germans when they ran away.
And he went back to this place, and he brought me two left shoes, two left boots.
That's all they-- they were there.
And he brought me the two left shoes, boots.
And I put in some paper inside, and I was ready all.
I felt better.
And we were liberated.
This was the night.
I just-- and it is very hard to explain, the night of the liberation.
The screaming from the people, the kissing from each other.
And then it dawned on you, who are you?
Whom do you have?
Where should you go?
At what point did you have any feeling about finding your family, or what had happened--
That was later.
That was a little later.
And then we have a-- we had an uncle, my husband's uncle.
And he said, we better run, because maybe the-- the Russians will move back and the Germans will come back, so we better start going with the Russians.
So we start going toward Poland.
We took our belongings.
[LAUGHS] We-- there we saw some what the Germans had, like a magazine.
So they had-- what you make shoes, you know, in their magazines.
Leather.
And flour.
Flour we didn't take.
And they had bread.
But really, we-- there was people.
What-- what-- the doctors were saying please don't eat, because people were dying after the war more every day than the last days in the war.
People were so hungry.
The Russian came in-- then the Russians came in, and they brought us hard-boiled eggs and everything.
And people started eating.
And they got diarrhea.
And a lot-- as a matter of fact, our uncle passed-- was-- died that time, too.
And people died left and right.
So we didn't eat too much.
We just ate a piece of bread what we wanted.
And they gave us coffee with sugar.
And we started going toward home.
[SIGHS] We walked eight days.
We had really a horse, but we put the-- the things what we stole from the Germans on this horse.
The Germans had a horse there too, because they were working with them.
We took the horse.
We had to push the horse, really, to go.
It was a little wagon, and an old horse.
We put our belongings what we stole, and we started going toward home.
At night, we went into a Polack and he gave-- let us sleep.
He was afraid for us.
And we slept too.
And when we came-- finally, we came to [INAUDIBLE].
Because--
Eight days we were going.
We walked.
You are still alive?
They killed so many, and they didn't kill you?
This was our hello.
Not at all.
Not at all.
Not at all.
No.
None-- none of them.
They made pogroms.
In Poland.
And they killed [? four ?] of the people one night.
We should run away the next morning, and we did.
After the war.
After the war, my husband had another uncle, and he went with him to-- he was living in a big town in Poland.
So he-- they started work, and he was very rich before the war, so he figured maybe he'll take something back from what he had.
So my husband went with him.
I was left with his three brothers.
Two brothers, because one was in Germany, and I didn't know him yet.
And we left.
We didn't have nothing to eat.
And the one, what he was-- after the war, we had nothing to eat.
Yes.
Yeah.
Yes.
And my husband went to Lodz where the uncle lived.
They started going, and maybe they-- they-- he checked on the way some place, and they were gone.
And I was-- and he said he'll come back.
And I was left with his brother, and-- with this two brothers.
And one brother went with another man [? where he lived. ?] He was-- before the war, he was sewing.
So he said, come with me.
I'll tell this man I'll sew for him, and you'll help me.
So he went.
And he went to a farmer.
He was sewing for him.
And the farmer just gave him to eat what he could eat.
And then mine brother-in-law, the one where he passed away, he said, my teeth are hurting.
I'm gonna just eat the inside from the bread, and the outside I'll take for tomorrow.
And he brought the outside, the hard pieces for me.
And that's how there was after the war.
And finally, after they killed the five people, my-- my husband was already back.
He left the uncle there.
We all packed the things, and we went to-- where did he tell you?
A different town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, my god.
[INAUDIBLE].
It'll come back to you.
It'll come back.
Sosnowiec.
Nothing.
No, because he was-- he'll tell the story.
He was several times back.
He-- he took some stuff back.
That way he has those pictures.
And-- no, nothing was.
His home was standing, I don't know.
We didn't live in his house.
We lived in another house.
No.
We married in '46.
This was in '45.
And in Sosnowiec, my husband was already doing some black marketing, went back to the-- to Germans towns.
There was already-- the war was finished.
We-- this was already after May.
And in May, the war finished.
And we had better then.
We had already something to eat, and life was better.
Then we decided we want to emigrate.
So--
Well, we didn't know it.
We wanted to leave Poland, because Poland was no good for us.
So we heard about it that, if you are in Germany, you can emigrate wherever you wanted.
So we started smuggling the border from Germany to-- from Poland to Czechoslovakia, and then to Germany.
And when we came to Germany, we were in a displaced camp.
First in [INAUDIBLE], then later in Deggendorf.
I knew right away-- I-- yes, I went-- well, then when I came to-- to this town, what I just said it--
Sosnowiec.
Oh, gee.
I'm so nervous.
Terrible.
When I came to Sosnowiec, I met a man from [INAUDIBLE].
For some reason, he came to buy something in a bigger town to bring it back to [INAUDIBLE] where the people needed [? the-- ?] there was a black market going on.
And he said to me that he goes on trains, and he can take me back.
So I'll see if I-- he doesn't know if I have someone or not.
And I knew that I had a house.
And I went back.
I went back, and I got so sick.
Didn't find anybody.
The house was living there a family.
And she asked me, are you alive?
He says, is a lot of you alive?
And she-- he didn't let me even to look at the house.
He said I pay rent, and it's not yours.
Who was he paying rent to?
To the Polish government.
That's what he said.
And I got so disgusted.
And I told him, I said, well, I lost everybody, I may as well lose the house, too.
And after three days, I went back to Sosnowiec.
Yeah, they all knew that this-- this transport was going to Treblinka and none of them survived, because it was not a camp, it was a finishing camp.
It was a death camp.
And this was the transport where I jumped from.
[BACKROUND NOISE]
We went to Germany.
And my-- and also, so we didn't work much, but I registered from wanting to go to Israel.
No, that time wasn't an Israel yet.
Yeah, it was-- yeah, a lot of people wanted to go.
They'll fight.
And in 1946, I got married.
It was beautiful, because there was the first wedding in this camp.
There was a few thousand people in the camp, and I was the first one to get married.
And we brought fish, and we-- we-- we killed a cow for the wedding.
And everybody who was with us in camp, from Tschenstochau, they heard about it, and they brought us back.
And they came to see us.
And we had a wedding, maybe 600 people.
It was a beautiful wedding.
But everybody cried.
We made it very tragic.
[SIGHS] When we walked into the wedding--
It was in Deggendorf, in a very big place, like entertaining hall.
We put two little tables-- [SOBBING] from both sides with the names of his parents and with the name of my parents.
And a black tablecloth on the top.
This was the only tragic thing.
Otherwise, it was very beautiful.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we got married.
I would tell you it was a chaplain, which I'm really looking for him.
I would like to find him.
I don't know his name.
It was an American chaplain.
He came, he married us.
He was a chaplain, so he was a rabbi.
He was crying like anything, because under the chuppah, everybody was crying.
And we married, and we are happy, [LAUGHS] very happy, till today's day.
Two years later, we had a baby, Shirley.
And then Israel was born, just to the date in April.
We came out-- Shirley was born in April, and Israel was born in May.
Yeah.
Well, when I was pregnant--
When I was pregnant, I tried to go to Israel.
And we have still the old papers.
And I became pregnant, so they didn't let us go.
This was the ship where we supposed to go on it.
A lot of our relatives went on it.
And they stayed in-- in Cyprus for quite a while, until they let them go to Israel.
You know that.
And when they came to Israel, they had such a rough time.
They didn't have nothing to eat, and where to sleep.
And so they wrote us a letter we shouldn't come.
But at that time, Shirley was born.
And--
This was in Deggendorf.
It was still a displaced person camp, yeah.
There was really a black market or whatever.
We were taking stuff from this town and bringing to the 'nother, and making a living, really, [? have ?] [? the ?] money and buying stuff.
Life was much better in the-- in the-- in the-- we were sent things from-- from America.
Not-- nobody.
But the organizations.
Red Cross or-- or the [INAUDIBLE].
And it wasn't too bad.
And then we registered to go to America.
And-- if we didn't go to Israel, already my daughter was 17 months old, we got the visa.
And we came, and we went.
My brother-in-law, the youngest, what I take his potatoes, he went before, because he went with the children.
Special children's quota.
So he went with the kids.
And he settled-- they taught him in [INAUDIBLE] in Rochester how to make-- how to work with his hands or to work-- to be electrician.
He went to school-- like I think he went to a-- a school what [? it ?] made by-- all right.
He went to school.
Yeah, a training school.
And he learned, and he was in Rochester.
So we registered to go to Rochester.
And that's how we came to Rochester.
He was in a-- he was in a-- in a-- all right, I just know.
[INAUDIBLE].
No, not in [INAUDIBLE].
In a technical school, yeah.
[INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] school.
It's terrible.
Yes.
Oh, yes, there was a hospital.
No, we went to the city hospital.
But it was a hospital for people when they took sick.
But like having a baby and like a different sickness, what they couldn't do it, they-- we went to the hospital, yeah.
We came.
Yeah.
To America.
It felt wonderful.
It's the most beautiful country what you can't imagine.
First of all, your freedom.
We really love it.
[SIGHS] Nobody appreciates freedom, I think, more than us.
This is the most precious thing in life, where the person can have his freedom.
It's worth-- it-- it is worth fighting for.
We came in here, and my husband worked by constructions all his-- all his life.
You'll see he worked in camp.
And that's how he learned.
But first, he worked in [INAUDIBLE].
And he made already $28 a week.
And it was a lot then.
I was cooking and baking.
But we still didn't have a place where to live.
We lived in a two-room apartment by somebody, like a sublet [INAUDIBLE].
Sub-- how do you say it?
A sublet?
A boarder.
Like a boarder.
Yeah.
And we lived there maybe for quite a few months.
She was pretty-- she was a nice lady, older lady.
And she had a big house.
And she subletted us two bedrooms, me and my brother-in-law.
And-- and they both worked, and we put away some money.
And my husband was making a little bit money, and later they gave us a thing, the Germans gave us some money, and we bought a house.
But this was already in 1947 or '48.
Oh, yeah, people were very-- this I cannot say.
We got to Rochester, and I-- we were sick.
And the-- the doctors didn't take no money.
And people tried to help us.
And he was ?] really nice.
They brought in-- soon we came, they brought in things for the baby.
But I was really ashamed to take, because I knew that, in Europe, if you took something, you were so poor.
And I didn't want it they should think that I'm so poor.
So I didn't want to take it, but they told me that it's nice.
And they brought everything for the baby.
And we used to go to shows, to-- to-- to see a movie.
And they used to give us dishes.
So they all went to the movies, and they got dishes, and they gave it to me.
I should have a whole set.
They really were very, very nice.
I cannot say no.
Then I went to work, too.
No, no, I was-- in the beginning, I was at home.
And when Shirley was six and started school, I went to work.
And I--
I worked in an optical company.
And I made very nice money, $50 a month-- a week, which it was a lot of money.
And I worked there for maybe eight months.
And then I became pregnant with the second child.
And I was pregnant [INAUDIBLE].
And I-- and I had-- I was very sick, because I didn't go to the-- I knew that I'm pregnant, and I didn't go to a doctor.
And finally, under two months, the tube busted, and I was poisoned all over.
And they took me to the hospital.
I was four weeks in the hospital.
And they had to change all my blood.
And all the newcomers came to the hospital day after day to give blood.
And on the third week, [? it ?] [? worked, ?] [? too. ?] And I-- but I didn't become pregnant anymore.
And that's why I have one child.
